a: The prose prologue. Job is introduced as blameless, upright, and exceedingly prosperous; in the heavenly court the Accuser charges that Job fears God only for his blessings. God permits the Accuser to strip Job of his wealth, his ten children, and finally his health, yet Job does not curse God, saying "the LORD gave, and the LORD has taken away." His three friends—Eliphaz, Bildad, and Zophar—arrive and sit silently with him in his grief.
summary: The prose prologue stages the heavenly test and Job's losses; though stripped of wealth, children, and health, Job worships and does not sin.

a: The poetic dialogue. Job breaks his silence by cursing the day of his birth, launching three cycles of speeches with his friends. Eliphaz, Bildad, and Zophar each insist that suffering proves hidden sin and urge Job to repent, while Job protests his innocence, laments God's apparent hostility, and longs for a mediator or vindicator. The exchange grows sharper as the friends' retribution theology fails to fit Job's case; chapters 29–31 close with Job's final defense, recalling his former honor and swearing an oath of integrity.
summary: Three cycles of dialogue in which the friends argue suffering proves sin while Job maintains his innocence and pleads for vindication.

a: The Elihu speeches. A younger bystander, Elihu, angrily breaks in, frustrated with both Job's self-justification and the friends' failure to answer him. Across four speeches he argues that God speaks through dreams and through suffering itself, using affliction to discipline and instruct rather than merely to punish. He exalts God's justice, greatness, and the wonder of the natural world, anticipating the storm and the divine speeches that follow.
summary: The young Elihu rebukes both Job and his friends, arguing God uses suffering to discipline and instruct, magnifying God's justice and majesty.

a: The LORD answers and the epilogue. God speaks from the whirlwind, never explaining Job's suffering but overwhelming him with questions about creation, the cosmos, and untamable creatures like Behemoth and Leviathan—displaying a wisdom and power Job cannot grasp. Job repents in dust and ashes, content to trust the God he has now "seen." In the prose epilogue God rebukes the three friends, has Job pray for them, and restores Job's fortunes twofold with new children.
summary: God answers from the whirlwind with creation's mysteries, Job repents, and the LORD restores his fortunes twofold.

a: Book I, dominated by psalms attributed to David. It opens with Psalm 1 (the two ways of the righteous and the wicked) and Psalm 2 (the LORD's anointed King), a paired introduction to the whole Psalter. The book is rich in individual laments and cries for deliverance, alongside psalms of trust and thanksgiving. Famous psalms here include Psalm 8 (humanity crowned with glory), Psalm 19 (the heavens declare God's glory and his law), Psalm 22 (the suffering one forsaken, quoted at the cross), and Psalm 23 (the LORD my shepherd).
summary: Book I gathers mostly Davidic psalms—laments, trust, and thanksgiving—introduced by the two ways of Psalm 1 and the anointed King of Psalm 2.

a: Book II includes the Korahite collection, more Davidic psalms, and one of Asaph. National and communal concerns come to the fore alongside personal devotion. Notable psalms include Psalm 42–43 (thirsting for God), Psalm 45 (a royal wedding song), Psalm 46 ("God is our refuge and strength"), Psalm 51 (David's penitential confession after Bathsheba), and the messianic Psalm 72 (the righteous reign of the king). The book closes by noting that "the prayers of David" are ended.
summary: Book II blends Korahite and Davidic psalms of longing, refuge, and royal hope, including the great penitential Psalm 51.

a: Book III is the shortest book, opening with a block of Asaph psalms and including more Korahite songs. Its tone is often dark and communal, wrestling with the prosperity of the wicked, the destruction of the temple, and apparent national defeat. Psalm 73 grapples with envy of the wicked until the worshiper enters the sanctuary; Psalm 88 is the bleakest lament in the Psalter; and Psalm 89 mourns the seeming failure of God's covenant with David, ending the book on a note of unresolved crisis.
summary: Book III, mostly Asaph and Korah, wrestles in communal lament with national disaster and the apparent failure of the Davidic covenant.

a: Book IV responds to Book III's crisis by reaffirming that the LORD reigns, not the failed earthly king. It opens with Psalm 90 (a prayer of Moses on human frailty and God's eternity) and features the great enthronement psalms (93, 95–99) proclaiming "The LORD reigns." It also includes Psalm 91 (refuge under God's wings), Psalm 100 (a summons to joyful worship), and Psalm 103 ("Bless the LORD, O my soul"). It closes with historical psalms recounting God's faithfulness amid Israel's rebellion.
summary: Book IV answers the Davidic crisis by enthroning the LORD as King, framed by Moses' Psalm 90 and the great "The LORD reigns" psalms.

a: Book V is a sweeping book of praise and thanksgiving for redemption. It includes the messianic Psalm 110 (the priest-king at God's right hand), the Egyptian Hallel (113–118) sung at Passover, the massive Psalm 119 in praise of God's law, the Songs of Ascents (120–134) for pilgrimage to Jerusalem, and Psalm 136 with its refrain "his steadfast love endures forever." The whole Psalter crescendos in the final Hallelujah psalms (146–150), ending with everything that has breath praising the LORD.
summary: Book V is a redemptive crescendo of praise—Psalm 110, the Hallel, Psalm 119, the Songs of Ascents—climaxing in the final Hallelujah psalms.

a: The extended prologue and call to wisdom. A father appeals to his son to embrace wisdom and the fear of the LORD, which is "the beginning of knowledge." These chapters personify Wisdom as a woman crying out in the streets, contrast her with the seductive folly of the adulteress and Woman Folly, and warn against violent companions, laziness, and sexual sin. The section climaxes with the rival banquets of Lady Wisdom and Lady Folly, framing the whole book as a choice between two paths.
summary: An extended father-to-son appeal that personifies Wisdom and Folly as rival women and grounds knowledge in the fear of the LORD.

a: The first main collection of the "proverbs of Solomon" (10:1–22:16). The flowing discourse of the prologue gives way to hundreds of short, mostly two-line sayings, many built on antithetical contrast between the righteous and the wicked, the wise and the foolish, the diligent and the lazy. They address speech, work, wealth and poverty, honesty, friendship, anger, and family life, offering compact observations on how godly wisdom plays out in everyday conduct.
summary: The core Solomonic collection of short, often antithetical sayings on speech, work, wealth, and character in daily life.

a: This range gathers the "sayings of the wise" (continuing from 22:17) and a second Solomonic collection "copied by the men of Hezekiah" (chapters 25–29). The proverbs broaden in form, including longer admonitions and vivid images. They warn against drunkenness, envy of the wicked, and flattery; counsel restraint, humility, and faithful friendship; and address rulers and justice. Memorable sayings here compare a fitting word to "apples of gold" and warn that returning to folly is like a dog returning to its vomit.
summary: The sayings of the wise plus Hezekiah's Solomonic collection, broadening into longer admonitions on self-control, justice, and folly.

a: The book's appendices, attributed to two otherwise unknown sages. Agur's words (chapter 30) are marked by humility before God's unsearchable wisdom and feature numerical sayings ("three things... four") observing the wonders and limits of creation. King Lemuel's mother's instruction (chapter 31) warns against women and wine that destroy kings, then closes the book with the acrostic poem of the noble "woman of valor," whose industry, wisdom, and fear of the LORD embody the wisdom the whole book has commended.
summary: The appendices of Agur and Lemuel, closing with the acrostic poem of the excellent wife who fears the LORD.

a: The Preacher (Qoheleth) frames his quest with the theme "vanity of vanities, all is vanity"—everything is fleeting and "a chasing after wind." He recounts testing life's pursuits—wisdom, pleasure, wealth, great building projects, and labor—and finds that none yields lasting gain "under the sun," since death levels the wise and the fool alike. He observes a time for everything under heaven and the injustices that mar life, concluding that the ability to enjoy food, work, and life is itself a gift from God.
summary: The Preacher tests wisdom, pleasure, and toil and finds all fleeting under the sun, commending the God-given enjoyment of ordinary life.

a: The Preacher turns to practical wisdom for living within life's limits and uncertainties. He weighs the value of wisdom over folly while admitting its limits, counsels reverence before God and submission to authority, and urges enjoying life's good gifts because the future is unknown and death is certain. The book climaxes with a poetic call to "remember your Creator in the days of your youth" before old age and death arrive, and concludes that the whole duty of humanity is to fear God and keep his commandments.
summary: Practical wisdom for life's limits, climaxing in the call to remember your Creator and the conclusion to fear God and keep his commandments.

a: The opening movement of the love poetry. The bride and her beloved exchange passionate praise of one another's beauty, longing for each other's presence and delighting in mutual love. The setting moves through vineyards, gardens, and springtime, with the refrain charging the daughters of Jerusalem not to awaken love before it pleases. The section builds toward the bride's nighttime search for her beloved and the grand royal wedding procession of King Solomon.
summary: The lovers exchange mutual praise and longing, moving toward the bride's night search and Solomon's wedding procession.

a: The love deepens toward consummation and mature commitment. The beloved extols the bride's beauty and calls her his "garden," and the union of the lovers is celebrated. A second episode of nighttime seeking and renewed praise follows, with the lovers affirming belonging to one another. The Song reaches its climax in chapter 8 with one of Scripture's great statements on love—that "love is strong as death" and "many waters cannot quench love"—portraying covenant love as a fierce, unquenchable flame.
summary: The lovers' union and mature commitment deepen, climaxing in the declaration that love is strong as death and cannot be quenched.
